title: Calendar Features: Time Metadata
concept: Calendar features extract patterns from date/time metadata (like day of the week, month, or holidays) to help models understand cyclical human behaviors.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between a lag feature and a rolling window feature?

options.text: A lag feature looks at a single past step, while a rolling window summarizes multiple past steps.
A lag feature only works on weekends, while rolling windows work on weekdays.
A rolling window size grows forever, while a lag feature is fixed.

exercises.explain: Correct! Lag features extract a single historical point, whereas rolling windows calculate statistics (like average) over a range of past steps.

cards.title: The weekend rush
cards.body: If you run an amusement park, you know that attendance is always much higher on Saturdays than on Tuesdays. A simple lag or rolling average feature might notice a sudden rise on Saturday, but it won't understand *why*. To help the model anticipate these patterns, we need to teach it how humans divide time.

cards.title: What are Calendar Features?
cards.body: Calendar features (or time metadata) are variables extracted from timestamps. They represent cycles like the day of the week (1 to 7), month of the year (1 to 12), hour of the day (0 to 23), or binary indicators (0 or 1) for holidays and weekends.

cards.title: A school planner
cards.body: Imagine you have a calendar with exams marked on it. You don't just study based on what you did yesterday; you study because the calendar says 'Monday' and you know school starts then. Calendar features give models a planner, so they know which day of the cycle it is.

cards.title: Electricity demand
cards.body: Electricity grids use calendar features to predict power usage. They extract Is_Weekend and Hour_of_Day. The model learns that power usage drops on weekends (since offices are closed) and peaks around 6 PM (when people get home and turn on appliances).

cards.title: Numbers aren't always linear
cards.body: You might think a model can easily read month numbers like 12 (December) and 1 (January). However, to a model, 12 is very far from 1, even though December is right next to January. Sometimes we must encode these numbers carefully, or split them into simple categories, so the model understands they form a circle.

cards.title: Extracting date details
cards.body: How a timestamp is converted into calendar features for a model:

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a calendar feature?

options.text: A flag indicating if the day is a weekend (0 or 1).
The average sales of the last 7 days.
The sales value from exactly 24 hours ago.
The overall forecast error of the model.

exercises.explain: Correct! Weekend flags are extracted directly from calendar metadata to capture weekly human behavior cycles.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Calendar features are useful for time series because human activities (like shopping or commuting) naturally follow weekly, monthly, and yearly cycles.
exercises.explain: True! Human cycles strongly influence time series data like retail sales, traffic, and energy usage.

exercises.sentence: To capture daily traffic patterns, a model should extract the ___ feature from the timestamp.
exercises.explain: The hour of the day is crucial for daily traffic patterns since rush hour happens at specific times.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a calendar feature that helps predict ice cream sales?

options.text: A feature indicating the month of the year (e.g., July vs. December).
The average temperature of the last 3 days.

exercises.explain: Correct! The month captures yearly seasonal cycles like summer vs. winter.

exercises.prompt: Why is a holiday flag (like 1 for Thanksgiving, 0 for other days) an important calendar feature?

options.text: Because holidays cause sudden, unusual changes in human behavior (like store closures or travel spikes) that standard daily patterns cannot explain.
Because holidays make the model run faster.
Because holidays are always the warmest days of the year.
Because models cannot train on days when people are not working.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Holidays create significant anomalies in standard cycles. By marking them with a flag, the model can learn to expect a major drop or spike on those specific days. Next: what if the memory window needs to grow over time?
